item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I can see where this is going, but it seems to lack substance. I just wish there was more.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"Holy $iht that's some nightmare fuel."
Said that out loud.
Came here from 4028, stayed for eating people like you eat lobster.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I believe this would be under the Rewrite Team's purview. Modern_Erasmus would be the first point of contact for this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I would like to underline this 2 year old comment, because it remains a really jarring mistake.
Memes just don't exert force. And since it can also prey on non-sapient animals (which are generally immune to memes by virtue of not having the capacity to understand them), "memetic" is doubly wrong.

Also, the third paragraph says:

SCP-1013 will only consume living tissue, and will ignore dead or decomposing flesh.

But then concludes by saying it will sometimes wait for prey to die and start to decay before feeding.
Those are mutually exclusive and one needs to meet the cutting room floor.

The first is more horror-worthy; but the second is closer to how IRL predators like Komodo Dragons feed. So either choice would have merit.

item.title: Blegh
item.content:encoded: Once I figured out what it was, I got excited-and then horribly let down by the lack of toads and poop. Still an upvote from me, though.

item.title: Oxford Comma
item.content:encoded:
SCP-1013 hunts by projecting a form of unknown radiation, wave or memetic force into prey items making eye contact with SCP-1013.

This tripped me up right off the bat simply for want of that Oxford Comma.

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
Timer Expired. Edits made as discussed below. Author may revert at any time.

The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team

+ Show Block
- Hide Block

Ah yes, the cockatrice. At the end of this article there is a segment that denotes the observed behaviors of juvenile instances of 1013. One such behavior is "Juvenile SCP-1013 will seek out cool, dark places and begin rapid molting, doubling in size every six hours until reaching full adult size." Another SCP article, namely SCP-238, directly mentions such behavior as part of its documentation. Its seems natural, given how 238 is basically the perfect molting grounds for 1013, this article directly link back there as well. As such, I am proposing the following edits:

Changing this:

Juvenile SCP-1013 will seek out cool, dark places and begin rapid molting, doubling in size every six hours until reaching full adult size. New adult SCP-1013 will quickly establish territories, and begin the feeding/spawning cycle again. Juvenile SCP-1013 seek out areas such as ventilation shafts, plumbing, or discarded clothing/shoes in which to molt and grow. Disturbing a juvenile during this period will always provoke a sustained attack.

To this:

Juvenile SCP-1013 will seek out cool, dark places and begin rapid molting, doubling in size every six hours until reaching full adult size. New adult SCP-1013 will quickly establish territories, and begin the feeding/spawning cycle again. Juvenile SCP-1013 seek out areas such as ventilation shafts, plumbing, or discarded clothing/shoes in which to molt and grow. Disturbing a juvenile during this period will always provoke a sustained attack.

If anyone has other/better examples that could be used here please leave a comment saying so!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Now I know this is a relatively old SCP, so current standards weren't necessarily applied at the time of writing, but one thing about this one really jumped out to me as incorrect.

SCP-1013 hunts by projecting a form of unknown radiation, wave or memetic force into prey items

Understanding Memetics is very quick to drill into people's heads that "Memetic SCPs do not emanate auras or project beams" and that "Memetics is not a thing that emanates in an aura."

I figure it can probably just be changed to "cognitohazardous" or something since you have to sense 1013's eyes to be affected, but I'm not 100% familiar with the terminology myself, so I'm not quite comfortable making an edit.

item.title: Staff Post - Reversion Notice
item.content:encoded: Version 25 of this article was reverted, as it removed words and introduced errors without author permission.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: As long as the containment procedures are followed there shouldn't be an issue so, why keter?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: i do like this one, but my main question would be "what exactly makes this a keter class?"

item.title: Image Attribution
item.content:encoded: The image used in this article is from Flickr, made by Mark Coggins, used here with his permission.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Man, one of the classics. This shit is freaky.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I love the concept, and +1'd for it, but this doesn't seem Keter to me. The paralyzing and calcification effects are easily countered by not looking at it and wearing armor, respectively. An infestation could be stopped by one person with a decent suit of armor and some sort of display. Even without that, you're safe as long as you put a wall between it and you.

If you want to make it Keter, make the calcification work on anything, or something like that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah I really could not be scared of this after seeing that picture. Still, good SCP well done.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Because this are resources they could recycle so that they have extra to build said "box" to hold a bigger, badder SCP, that's why. The SCP foundation is anything but wasteful. I mean, yes, they CAN make a box on their own, why waste the resources when this thing is practically giving free supplies.

But on a side note: shouldn't this be Euclid? Wasn't the original def. for Euclid animal-like or primal? And this thing hardly poses a global threat, I mean it poses a threat, but no way it'd be global, even with it's babies.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Love the staredown in the pic. The description of hunting/feeding habits was great(ly disturbing) as well. BUT- the reproduction cycle is just plain unbelievable. Either these guys have a natural predator somewhere or all of Egypt should be crawling with them by now.

Also, if the stare effect is memetic, maybe the photographer was calcified while taking the picture. "Hahaha, you can't turn me to stone, I'm 400ft away with a zoom lens *stare* …REDACTED!"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Meh, that's just a regular cockatrice and I don't see how

SCP-1013 has been noted to use natural body openings (mouth and eyes, primarily) when available, and is capable of compressing its body to fit in to very small openings.

JESUS CHRIST NO

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Done! The "Cock" part was asterisked out in the edit box, so I'm wondering if it might have been a censor issue?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Go ahead.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Is there a reason the mainlist name for this is now just "atrice"? (I'm not sure how to fix it or if I even should)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I mean this one. The lizard whose body is shopped with a chicked head.

But apparently not, I'm confusing the lizards. Darn.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What. That's a lizard with a chicken head shooped onto it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Man, I love this pic. Isn't that a basilisk? I.e., the real lizard? That seems sort of wrong…

Nevermind that, it's awesome.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
……….. :D

*likes petrification monsters*

But now I'm curious as to what it tastes like. I doubt no one's tried; there's more than one of them, they look flimsy enough and you only have to worry if they actually bite you.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
ARGHHHHHHHHHHHHHHHHHHHHHHHHHHHHHHHH!

+1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I have to disagree. The photo gives you the initial WTF. Excepting minor lighting issues (impossible to avoid in most composites), I think it blends quite nicely into a mostly-believable form. The description takes it from there.

As to an earlier query, this fits fully into Keter for me, esp. with the reproduction. Effect range of 54m. Imagine what one of these loose in a high school, not discovered until the guys in the maintenance shed start hatching more, could do. Not only general risk to life in a suburban area, but incredible risk to the status quo. 'Cause some smartass gamer kid is gonna start calling it a Cockatrice in the media and…and…Oh YEAH. They'll spread fast.

Having read earlier iterations, this has come an amazingly long way. Very solid. But still not Bigfoot.

+1 while trying not to look at it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I just can't really see the picture as being real. The SCP is fantastic, but the picture I feel, falls short.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Mmm. I guess the calcification could create a…container?…for the liquefied yuck inside, if it worked like that. I didn't think it liquefied the non-calcified flesh though…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It could be a similar function to a spider cocooning its prey, turning the whole innards into a nasty mess that it can easily just remove and suck down… I imagine in this case it would just be a bit more… chunky…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What's the advantage to calcification? Why not just eat the paralyzed victim? It loses a lot of nutritious tissue to the calcification process.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Gears:

SCP-1013 hunts by projecting a form of unknown radiation, wave or memetic force into prey items making eye contact with SCP-1013.

Permission to edit this line? Memetics aren't a force (but rather transference of information).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I actually like this one. We have a Cain and an Able, SCP-343, an invisible woman, vampires (decommissioned), boogymen, succubi, statues that demand human sacrifice, monsters from various mythologies, etc, etc. A creature that the cockatrice of legend is based on (or an object created in the likeness of a cockatrice) is only logical.

To me, the horror is in those people who have been been transformed. They are still alive under that 3 cm of stone! Has anyone tried communicating with them? Have they tried chipping away the stone (a skinny person may not survive, but in someone with a lot of muscle of fat, the petrification may allow them to survive the removal)? Could SCP-500 cure them? (I am not suggesting this, just curious). Maybe the calcium comes from their bones, so they are just a liquified mess inside. An addendum from a survivor would be unnerving as hell.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think it's really cool that there's an explanation for the petrification, but other than that, it's just a cockatrice viewed from a scientific standpoint. Downvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
As I recall, an almost identical idea is played out in the article for SCP-098, except it's a fully original idea and not a new take on a mythical monster.
Unfortunately, i'm afraid that it is more fleshed out and concise and does fantastically well at leaving elements to your imagination. As such, i'm downvoting. Sorry.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I sympathize with this. Anything worth doing is worth doing well and all that, eh?

And I like the additions. Pretty interesting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
well…yeah, but I want to try and get back to the olden days. I had kind of a lame duck slump for a bit, and i'm trying to prove to myself that i still have something to say, SCP wise. Plus, I'm trying to see if I actually have any editing skills…

…

Yeah, and it's probably some personal vanity as well…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
i really think i can save this thing

It's +17; you do know that, right?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Edited. Again. It's odd, I normally feel that, if something needs a ton of edits, it's probably not worth it, but for some reason i really think i can save this thing. Added some notes at the end, let me know if it does anything for you.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really like it, very creative way of looking at the "turn to stone concept." I'm going to upvote it, but I don't really think it capture the SCP Foundation's whole "paranormal/creepy containable yet completely enigmatic" vibe. It feel's like it's really too understood, even though it tries to have a mysterious tone when trying to understand the calcification process and the radiation type. I really think it's a well written and well inspired SCP, but I don't think it deserves to be the 1000th one.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A minor comment: This probably shouldn't be fed so frequently. This seems to be an ordinary reptile with some unusual abilities. Reptiles need very little food. A rat every week is probably enough to keep it going just fine. Similarly, it seems a bit odd that it would freeze multiple humans. A human is enough food to keep it going for months. Either say something about a hyperaccelerated metabolism (which is pretty bad if you're cold blooded) or drop the frequency.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: …that might work, actually. I'll think on it…been hammering out some edits, we'll see what happens after those…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Attach a retrieval/containment breach log. This is classic movie monster shit right here- having to track and catch something you can't look at.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This just doesn't scare me, sorry.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This was inspired by My Little Pony? New favorite SCP backstory.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Why on earth would they do that? The Foundation most certainly has the resources to build a box.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Whatever is calcified is recycled and used in the construction of the thing's containment area.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Good god. This is fantastic. I could never figure out how to make a cockatrice into an SCP. Applause.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: They're incinerated; simple.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is pretty cool, but I think what it needs is a bit more terror. Keters tend to cost me some sleep. I can't tell if the language is too impersonal to be scary or not impersonal enough, or maybe that isn't even the issue. But yeah, a scare factor, maybe something that makes it harder to contain, along with a reason not to just kill it would be good. Because my first instinct is "kill it".
Maybe you could detail how the calcified staff are disposed of in the procedures, you could make that pretty unsettling.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: dang it, knew i missed something…did a lot of shuffling edits, and didn't catch the quote changes. I'll buff that a bit, and see if i can't tighten up the phrasing some more. I keep finding little things with this one…god help me, i might actually be getting alright at editing…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like this and upvoted it.

That being said, I found it a little odd than you use the term "calcification" several times early on, and then about half way down put it in quotes and treat it like a new word:

The outer skin tissues will begin a rapid “calcification”, growing very dense and inflexible over several minutes

I feel like you either need to define it earlier or just don't define it like that at all; I figured what you mean.

Other than that, this just feels a tad bit rough around the edges. I would like to see some of the phrasing smoothed out and the organization improved a bit.

This all just me being nitpicky however, overall I enjoyed this one.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: this actually helps a lot…I may try and crack this a bit, combine a few sections and see if i can add a bit to the horror without being too cheesy about it. At the moment though, i'm very tired, and i tend to ramble pretty bad when i'm tired, so…yeah. Still, this helps.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
If it makes it, i may clean it up more, if not, the world is none the worse…

Hey, entries to this contest that remain in the positives will also be given SCP slots (just only the highest rated will be given the actual SCP-1000 slot). So I say work on it now! Plenty of potential here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Just like with SCPs of real things, SCPs of real legends need to in some way be more interesting than the thing they're based on.

While I do agree this article could use something more, I would argue this is a bit more interesting than the mythical cockatrice. More horrifying, that's for sure.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like this, and I've upvoted. I really like the angle you've taken on the cockatrice; the calcification is pretty horrifying. And I'm a sucker for well-done myth-based SCPs. Plus there are plenty of little details in here that are great. Couple examples:

SCP-1000 has been noted to use natural body openings (mouth and eyes, primarily) on occasion

(dear god)

Subjects have responded poorly to questioning during feeding, resulting in a lack of direct data about this progression.

However…

This is simply not as memorable as I would like. The detail work here (and, of course, your previous work) tells me that this could be even better. Also, this is pretty long for the amount of horror we get out of it; it could stand to be both expanded upon (with more delightful creepy horror stuff) and cut down.

What's here already is enough for an upvote, but I also look forward to seeing where this goes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I have to agree. This is a professionally done article, but there just isn't much to it. The only things this adds to the legend is an explanation for the petrification, and the thing with the frill. The former makes so much sense that it just sinks without really being noted. The frill is clever and why I'm not downvoting this, but this isn't worth an upvote either.

Just like with SCPs of real things, SCPs of real legends need to in some way be more interesting than the thing they're based on.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I honestly have to agree…this is just a concept i've been thinking about for a long time, the whole "why petrify" question. If it makes it, i may clean it up more, if not, the world is none the worse…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: In suppose it's well written, but nothing about his article really sticks out. I feel like I could wake up in the morning and remember nothing about this article aside from the fact that it was a cockatrice. IMO, one of the main problems is that it's a cockatrice, which although combines well with tales of old when everybody gathered around a story-teller and listen to him or her shout and make scary voices, does not combine well with the clinical and scientific tone of the Foundation. Sooo… -1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
darn good, Gears. Now I just have to make sure my lizard legend doesn't seem too much like a copy when its finally done >.<

Still, 1 up.
